the SB outsole has the ribs not going to the midsole completely for a simple reason: as the rubber is pushed into the mould the barrier where both mid- and outsole material meet is better on the SBs because the ribs don't go fully to the edge. This design was an update from the OG dunk soles. As well as other aspects like the extra griplines on the bottom etc.

The Nike dunk was created in 1985 by the same team who developed the Jordan 1, the Nike dunk high was originally a basketball shoe, it suited college basketball teams as you could create so many colourways to fit the colours of the team. Nike was struggling to fit into the skate culture already having a failed team in the past when they signed a very young bam margera. And released 3 'skate shoes' which included the Nike choad.... it wasn't until they brought sandy bodecker to the team. To shape nike into what nike sb is today. To give back to local skateshops and give local shops a chance to collaborate on shoes, nike would also make sure that the shoes were sold by skaters, for skaters. And not sold at other retail shops. Such as footlocker or tkmax. In my opinion the dunk created the stepping stone for street wear and resellers. Camp outs at shop fronts to get a fresh pair of dunks. It wasn't all down to nike sb, and collaborations. While on the subject, I can't not talk about the impact that Jeff staple had with nike and the reselling market. When he first released the pigeon dunk in March 2005.

This is a good starting point for someone barely getting into Dunks. I will say tho this is the tip of the iceberg, because there’s a bunch of other little things to keep in mind. Like every SB before 2011ish looks more like the normal dunk, but with a fat tongue. The flap on the laces with the redesigned outsole didn’t exist till around that time. Also more recently Nike SB started letting the brands they collab with use the “old” Dunk SB design, so if you see newer SB Dunks and the design doesn’t match up with the Chicago pair he’s showing don’t get too spooked lol.

The Dunk Low has no collectible value. There was never a low-top dunk shoe. They turned the dunk into a tennis show (that is what a low-top is, a tennis shoe...not a basketball shoe) in the 2000's when they retroed it. The Dunk SB DOES hold collector value though, as that is a spin-off and basically a different shoe in it''s own. It is mean to be a skateboard shoe (that is the SB). If you don't like the feel of a basketball shoe you can buy a low-top, but collector's don't want them if they were originally meant to be a basketball shoe.
